1.synthesis: Proverbs 22:1–16 closes the main Solomonic collection (which began at Proverbs 10:1) with a set of capstone observations about reputation, generational instruction, and the social consequences of character. The opening proverb establishes the chapter’s value framework: “A good name is to be chosen rather than great riches, and favor is better than silver or gold” (v. 1). The theological leveling of v. 2 (“the rich and the poor meet together; the LORD is the maker of them all”) makes common creatureliness the ground of social ethics. The most widely memorized verse of the entire Solomonic collection appears here: “Train up a child in the way he should go; even when he is old he will not depart from it” (v. 6). The section closes with the observation that YHWH himself acts against the crooked: “The mouth of forbidden women is a deep pit; he with whom the LORD is angry will fall into it” (v. 14), and the positive counterpart: the king’s favor for the man of “gracious speech” (v. 11).
1.historical_context: The honor-economy of the ancient Near East made reputation (shem ṭov, a good name) the primary social currency. In a world without credit bureaus or formal contracts, a good name was the practical foundation for commerce, patronage, and marriage alliances. Verse 2’s theological leveling counters the ANE tendency to read social stratification as divinely ordained: Mesopotamian texts often portrayed the gods as creating humans in different classes for different purposes. Proverbs asserts that the LORD is the maker of both rich and poor — a creation-grounded social ethic. The “train up a child” (v. 6, chânôkh lannâ‘ar, dedicate/initiate the youth) reflects the Hebrew practice of formal induction into a life-path or craft, with the implication that the formation laid down early persists. The term chânôkh is related to the dedication of a building or altar (Numbers 7:10), suggesting that child-formation is an act of consecration as much as instruction.
1.literary_structure: Proverbs 22:1–16 functions as the epilogue of the main Solomon collection. The capstone quality is visible in the density of the “better than” wisdom: a good name over riches (v. 1); humility and fear of the LORD over mere wealth (v. 4). The section includes practical warnings about the sluggard’s self-defeating logic (v. 13) and the rod of discipline removing folly from the child’s heart (v. 15). The positive promise of v. 4 (“the reward for humility and fear of the LORD is riches and honor and life”) summarizes the entire collection’s vision of the outcome of wisdom-formation.
1.theological_application: “A good name is to be chosen rather than great riches” (v. 1) is not ascetic withdrawal from the material world but a clear-eyed account of what is genuinely valuable: the reputation for integrity is a more durable asset than accumulated wealth. The creation-grounded social ethics of v. 2 make the exploitation of the poor a theological impossibility for the wise person, since the Maker of the poor is also the Maker of the rich. “Train up a child in the way he should go” (v. 6) grounds generational hope in early formation: the household is the primary site of wisdom-transmission, and parents who invest in that formation are investing in a permanence that outlasts childhood. The reward of humility and fear of the LORD (v. 4) summarizes what the entire collection from chapters 1 through 22 has been saying.
1.christology: “A good name is to be chosen rather than great riches” (v. 1) is fulfilled in the one whose name is above every name (Phil 2:9), who received his exalted name not by grasping but by self-emptying. The creation-leveling of rich and poor under one Maker (v. 2) becomes in Christ the community where “there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free” (Gal 3:28), the same creational truth enacted ecclesially. The training of a child in the way they should go (v. 6) points toward the one who himself was formed in wisdom and stature (Luke 2:52) and who is the Way in which all children of God are to be trained.

17.synthesis: Proverbs 22:17 inaugurates a qualitatively distinct section of the book: the “Thirty Sayings of the Wise” (v. 20), a structured collection of wisdom instructions that bears the closest formal resemblance in the Hebrew Bible to the Egyptian Instruction of Amenemope, a thirty-chapter wisdom text from approximately the thirteenth to eleventh centuries BC. The explicit instruction form — “Incline your ear, and hear the words of the wise, and apply your heart to my knowledge” (v. 17) — returns to the direct address of chs. 1–9 after the two-line sentence proverbs of chs. 10–22:16. The section is explicitly teleological: “that your trust may be in the LORD, I have made them known to you today, even to you” (v. 19). The sayings of the first group address justice for the poor (vv. 22–23), association with the angry (vv. 24–25), pledges and debt (vv. 26–27), the ancient boundary stone (v. 28), and skill as the path to royal standing (v. 29).
17.historical_context: The Instruction of Amenemope (discovered in 1888, published 1923) contains thirty chapters of wisdom instruction from an Egyptian court official, and its parallels with Proverbs 22:17–24:22 are so close that most scholars posit either direct literary dependence or a common ANE wisdom tradition. Whether the relationship is direct borrowing, Israelite appropriation, or parallel use of shared wisdom forms, the Thirty Sayings demonstrate that Israelite wisdom was not isolated but in conversation with the broader international wisdom tradition. This does not diminish the Torah-grounded character of the section: the explicit purpose statement is “that your trust may be in the LORD” (v. 19), and the content has been Yahwized throughout. The ancient boundary stone (gebul ‘olam, the landmark of eternity) of v. 28 connects to the law’s prohibition of boundary removal (Deuteronomy 19:14), grounding wisdom in covenant law.
17.literary_structure: Proverbs 22:17–29 marks the beginning of the Thirty Sayings collection that extends through Proverbs 24:22. The introduction (vv. 17–21) establishes the purpose, audience, and aim before the individual sayings begin. The sayings in this opening set are longer than the two-line antithetical proverbs of chs. 10–22:16: each saying is typically two to four verses long with an instruction, a reason, and sometimes a consequence. The explicit purpose (“that your trust may be in the LORD”) gives this section a more explicitly devotional aim than the sentence proverbs, framing wisdom not merely as practical skill but as the formation of trust (bitachon) in YHWH.
17.theological_application: “Do not rob the poor, because he is poor, or crush the afflicted at the gate” (v. 22) grounds the prohibition not in social preference but in a theological threat: “for the LORD will plead their cause and rob of life those who rob them” (v. 23). YHWH acts as the legal advocate (rib) for the poor against those who exploit them. The instruction to skill-development (v. 29, “do you see a man skilled in his work? He will stand before kings”) democratizes access to royal influence through excellence — not merely birth or wealth but diligent craft determines social elevation. The whole purpose of the Thirty Sayings (“that your trust may be in the LORD,” v. 19) makes explicit what was implicit in the entire collection: wisdom’s ultimate goal is not cleverness but trust.
17.christology: “Do not remove the ancient landmark that your fathers have set” (v. 28) points to YHWH’s own faithfulness to the ancient covenantal boundaries he set: Christ comes not to abolish but to fulfill (Matt 5:17), honoring the landmarks set by the fathers. YHWH’s advocacy for the poor (vv. 22–23) becomes in Christ a personal advocacy: he who “pleads their cause” is the one who himself stood as the advocate before the tribunal of God, taking on himself the condemnation so that those who are his might go free (1 John 2:1). The purpose of wisdom — “that your trust may be in the LORD” (v. 19) — is fulfilled when trust is placed in the one who is YHWH incarnate, the wisdom of God in person (1 Cor 1:24).
